entries.title: Label
entries.summary: Tags objects with identifying attributes that are meaningful and relevant to users.

entries.title: Probe
entries.summary: A check performed periodically by the kubelet on a container in a Pod.

entries.title: LimitRange
entries.summary: Provides constraints to limit resource consumption per Containers or Pods in a namespace.

entries.title: Dynamic Resource Allocation
entries.summary: A Kubernetes feature for requesting and sharing resources, like hardware accelerators, among Pods.

entries.title: Platform Developer
entries.summary: A person who customizes the Kubernetes platform to fit the needs of their project.

entries.title: Reviewer
entries.summary: A person who reviews code for quality and correctness on some part of the project.

entries.title: CustomResourceDefinition
entries.summary: Custom code that defines a resource to add to your Kubernetes API server without building a complete custom server.

entries.title: Upstream (disambiguation)
entries.summary: May refer to: core Kubernetes or the source repo from which a repo was forked.

entries.title: Service Catalog
entries.summary: A former extension API that enabled applications running in Kubernetes clusters to easily use external managed software offerings, such as a datastore service offered by a cloud provider.

entries.title: PodGroup
entries.summary: A PodGroup represents a set of Pods with common scheduling policy and constraints.

entries.title: Admission Controller
entries.summary: A piece of code that intercepts requests to the Kubernetes API server prior to persistence of the object.

entries.title: Cluster Operations
entries.summary: The work involved in managing a Kubernetes cluster.

entries.title: Persistent Volume Claim
entries.summary: Claims storage resources defined in a PersistentVolume so that it can be mounted as a volume in a container.

entries.title: Contributor
entries.summary: Someone who donates code, documentation, or their time to help the Kubernetes project or community.

entries.title: Endpoints
entries.summary: (Deprecated) API representing endpoints of a Service

entries.title: Immutable Infrastructure
entries.summary: Immutable Infrastructure refers to computer infrastructure (virtual machines, containers, network appliances) that cannot be changed once deployed

entries.title: ConfigMap
entries.summary: An API object used to store non-confidential data in key-value pairs. Can be consumed as environment variables, command-line arguments, or configuration files in a volume.

entries.title: Extensions
entries.summary: Extensions are software components that extend and deeply integrate with Kubernetes to support new types of hardware.

entries.title: etcd
entries.summary: Consistent and highly-available key value store used as backing store of Kubernetes for all cluster data.

entries.title: HostAliases
entries.summary: A HostAliases is a mapping between the IP address and hostname to be injected into a Pod's hosts file.

entries.title: Container network interface (CNI)
entries.summary: Container network interface (CNI) plugins are a type of Network plugin that adheres to the appc/CNI specification.

entries.title: Sidecar Container
entries.summary: An auxilliary container that stays running throughout the lifecycle of a Pod.

entries.title: DeviceClass
entries.summary: A category of devices in the cluster. Users can claim specific devices in a DeviceClass.

entries.title: API server
entries.summary: Control plane component that serves the Kubernetes API.

entries.title: Volume
entries.summary: A directory containing data, accessible to the containers in a pod.

entries.title: ResourceClaim
entries.summary: Describes the resources that a workload needs, such as devices. ResourceClaims can request devices from DeviceClasses.

entries.title: Node-pressure eviction
entries.summary: Node-pressure eviction is the process by which the kubelet proactively fails pods to reclaim resources on nodes.

entries.title: Preemption
entries.summary: Preemption logic in Kubernetes helps a pending Pod to find a suitable Node by evicting low priority Pods existing on that Node.

entries.title: Container
entries.summary: A lightweight and portable executable image that contains software and all of its dependencies.

entries.title: Cluster
entries.summary: A set of worker machines, called nodes, that run containerized applications. Every cluster has at least one worker node.

entries.title: Aggregation Layer
entries.summary: The aggregation layer lets you install additional Kubernetes-style APIs in your cluster.

entries.title: Master
entries.summary: Legacy term, used as synonym for nodes running the control plane.

entries.title: Code Contributor
entries.summary: A person who develops and contributes code to the Kubernetes open source codebase.

entries.title: kube-controller-manager
entries.summary: Control Plane component that runs controller processes.

entries.title: CRI-O
entries.summary: A lightweight container runtime specifically for Kubernetes

entries.title: Secret
entries.summary: Stores sensitive information, such as passwords, OAuth tokens, and ssh keys.

entries.title: Downward API
entries.summary: A mechanism to expose Pod and container field values to code running in a container.

entries.title: Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF)
entries.summary: The Cloud Native Computing Foundation is an organization that promotes and supports the development of cloud-native technologies.

entries.title: Condition
entries.summary: A condition represents the current state of a Kubernetes resource, providing information about whether certain aspects of the resource are true.

entries.title: Application Architect
entries.summary: A person responsible for the high-level design of an application.

entries.title: kOps (Kubernetes Operations)
entries.summary: kOps will not only help you create, destroy, upgrade and maintain production-grade, highly available, Kubernetes cluster, but it will also provision the necessary cloud infrastructure.

entries.title: Container Environment Variables
entries.summary: Container environment variables are name=value pairs that provide useful information into containers running in a Pod.

entries.title: Downstream (disambiguation)
entries.summary: May refer to: code in the Kubernetes ecosystem that depends upon the core Kubernetes codebase or a forked repo.

entries.title: Network Policy
entries.summary: A specification of how groups of Pods are allowed to communicate with each other and with other network endpoints.

entries.title: Pod Priority
entries.summary: Pod Priority indicates the importance of a Pod relative to other Pods.

entries.title: Mixed Version Proxy (MVP)
entries.summary: Feature that lets a kube-apiserver proxy a resource request to a different peer API server.

entries.title: Image
entries.summary: Stored instance of a container that holds a set of software needed to run an application.

entries.title: Disruption
entries.summary: An event that leads to Pod(s) going out of service

entries.title: Device Plugin
entries.summary: Software extensions to let Pods access devices that need vendor-specific initialization or setup

entries.title: Shuffle-sharding
entries.summary: A technique for assigning requests to queues that provides better isolation than hashing modulo the number of queues.

entries.title: Toleration
entries.summary: A core object consisting of three required properties: key, value, and effect. Tolerations enable the scheduling of pods on nodes or node groups that have a matching taint.

entries.title: UID
entries.summary: A Kubernetes systems-generated string to uniquely identify objects.

entries.title: Pod Disruption
entries.summary: The process by which Pods on Nodes are terminated either voluntarily or involuntarily.

entries.title: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler
entries.summary: Object that automatically scales the number of pod replicas based on targeted resource utilization or custom metric targets.

entries.title: Cloud Provider
entries.summary: An organization that offers a cloud computing platform.

entries.title: Kubernetes API
entries.summary: The application that serves Kubernetes functionality through a RESTful interface and stores the state of the cluster.

entries.title: Cluster Operator
entries.summary: A person who configures, controls, and monitors clusters.

entries.title: Control Plane
entries.summary: The container orchestration layer that exposes the API and interfaces to define, deploy, and manage the lifecycle of containers.

entries.title: kube-proxy
entries.summary: `kube-proxy` is a network proxy that runs on each node in the cluster.

entries.title: JSON Web Token (JWT)
entries.summary: A means of representing claims to be transferred between two parties.

entries.title: Kubectl
entries.summary: A command line tool for communicating with a Kubernetes cluster.

entries.title: Pod Lifecycle
entries.summary: The sequence of states through which a Pod passes during its lifetime.

entries.title: CronJob
entries.summary: A repeating task (a Job) that runs on a regular schedule.

entries.title: Quantity
entries.summary: A whole-number representation of small or large numbers using SI suffixes.

entries.title: Application Developer
entries.summary: A person who writes an application that runs in a Kubernetes cluster.

entries.title: Dockershim
entries.summary: A component of Kubernetes v1.23 and earlier, which allows Kubernetes system components to communicate with Docker Engine.

entries.title: sysctl
entries.summary: An interface for getting and setting Unix kernel parameters

entries.title: Node
entries.summary: A node is a worker machine in Kubernetes.

entries.title: Watch
entries.summary: A verb that is used to track changes to an object in Kubernetes as a stream.

entries.title: ReplicationController
entries.summary: A (deprecated) API object that manages a replicated application.

entries.title: PriorityClass
entries.summary: A mapping from a class name to the scheduling priority that a Pod should have.

entries.title: FlexVolume
entries.summary: FlexVolume is a deprecated interface for creating out-of-tree volume plugins. The {{< glossary_tooltip text="Container Storage Interface" term_id="csi" >}} is a newer interface that addresses several problems with FlexVolume.

entries.title: DaemonSet
entries.summary: Ensures a copy of a Pod is running across a set of nodes in a cluster.

entries.title: Developer (disambiguation)
entries.summary: May refer to: Application Developer, Code Contributor, or Platform Developer.

entries.title: Feature gate
entries.summary: A way to control whether or not a particular Kubernetes feature is enabled.

entries.title: Operator pattern
entries.summary: A specialized controller used to manage a custom resource

entries.title: Drain
entries.summary: Safely evicts Pods from a Node to prepare for maintenance or removal.

entries.title: Name
entries.summary: A client-provided string that refers to an object in a resource URL, such as `/api/v1/pods/some-name`.

entries.title: EndpointSlice
entries.summary: EndpointSlices track the IP addresses of Pods for Services.

entries.title: Namespace
entries.summary: An abstraction used by Kubernetes to support isolation of groups of resources within a single cluster.

entries.title: ResourceSlice
entries.summary: Represents one or more infrastructure resources, like devices, in a pool of similar resources.

entries.title: Helm Chart
entries.summary: A package of pre-configured Kubernetes configurations that can be managed with the Helm tool.

entries.title: QoS Class
entries.summary: QoS Class (Quality of Service Class) provides a way for Kubernetes to classify pods within the cluster into several classes and make decisions about scheduling and eviction.

entries.title: PodTemplate
entries.summary: A template for creating Pods.

entries.title: Controller
entries.summary: A control loop that watches the shared state of the cluster through the apiserver and makes changes attempting to move the current state towards the desired state.

entries.title: Resource (infrastructure)
entries.summary: A defined amount of infrastructure available for consumption (CPU, memory, etc).

entries.title: Kubeadm
entries.summary: A tool for quickly installing Kubernetes and setting up a secure cluster.

entries.title: Istio
entries.summary: An open platform (not Kubernetes-specific) that provides a uniform way to integrate microservices, manage traffic flow, enforce policies, and aggregate telemetry data.

entries.title: containerd
entries.summary: A container runtime with an emphasis on simplicity, robustness and portability

entries.title: Kubelet
entries.summary: An agent that runs on each node in the cluster. It makes sure that containers are running in a pod.

entries.title: Garbage Collection
entries.summary: A collective term for the various mechanisms Kubernetes uses to clean up cluster resources.

entries.title: Dynamic Volume Provisioning
entries.summary: Allows users to request automatic creation of storage  Volumes.

entries.title: cAdvisor
entries.summary: Tool that provides understanding of the resource usage and performance characteristics for containers

entries.title: Object
entries.summary: An entity in the Kubernetes system, representing part of the state of your cluster.

entries.title: Ephemeral Container
entries.summary: A type of container type that you can temporarily run inside a Pod

entries.title: Volume Plugin
entries.summary: A Volume Plugin enables integration of storage within a Pod.

entries.title: ResourceClaimTemplate
entries.summary: Defines a template for Kubernetes to create ResourceClaims. Used to provide per-Pod or per-PodGroup access to separate, similar resources.

entries.title: Subject Alternative Name
entries.summary: An X.509 certificate extension to identify what hostname or IP address the certificate applies to.

entries.title: Manifest
entries.summary: A serialized specification of one or more Kubernetes API objects.

entries.title: Finalizer
entries.summary: A namespaced key that tells Kubernetes to wait until specific conditions are met before it fully deletes an object marked for deletion.

entries.title: cgroup (control group)
entries.summary: A group of Linux processes with optional resource isolation, accounting and limits.

entries.title: Deployment
entries.summary: Manages a replicated application on your cluster.

entries.title: Certificate
entries.summary: A cryptographically secure file used to validate access to the Kubernetes cluster.

entries.title: Glossary
entries.summary: A centralized repository of terms and definitions used within a project or organization.

entries.title: WG (working group)
entries.summary: Facilitates the discussion and/or implementation of a short-lived, narrow, or decoupled project for a committee, SIG, or cross-SIG effort.

entries.title: Logging
entries.summary: Logs are the list of events that are logged by cluster or application.

entries.title: CLA (Contributor License Agreement)
entries.summary: Terms under which a contributor grants a license to an open source project for their contributions.

entries.title: Cluster Infrastructure
entries.summary: The infrastructure layer provides and maintains VMs, networking, security groups and others.

entries.title: Taint
entries.summary: A core object consisting of three required properties: key, value, and effect. Taints prevent the scheduling of pods on nodes or node groups.

entries.title: Managed Service
entries.summary: A software offering maintained by a third-party provider.

entries.title: Static Pod
entries.summary: A pod managed directly by the kubelet daemon on a specific node.

entries.title: StatefulSet
entries.summary: A StatefulSet manages deployment and scaling of a set of Pods, with durable storage and persistent identifiers for each Pod.

entries.title: ResourceQuota
entries.summary: Provides constraints that limit aggregate resource consumption per namespace.

entries.title: user namespace
entries.summary: A Linux kernel feature to emulate superuser privilege for unprivileged users.

entries.title: Init Container
entries.summary: One or more initialization containers that must run to completion before any app containers run.

entries.title: Gateway API
entries.summary: An API for modeling service networking in Kubernetes.

entries.title: Event
entries.summary: Kubernetes objects that describe some state change in the cluster.

entries.title: Annotation
entries.summary: A key-value pair that is used to attach arbitrary non-identifying metadata to objects.

entries.title: API-initiated eviction
entries.summary: API-initiated eviction is the process by which you use the Eviction API to create an Eviction object that triggers graceful pod termination.

entries.title: Service
entries.summary: A way to expose an application running on a set of Pods as a network service.

entries.title: Security Context
entries.summary: The securityContext field defines privilege and access control settings for a Pod or container.

entries.title: Eviction
entries.summary: Process of terminating one or more Pods on Nodes

entries.title: Minikube
entries.summary: A tool for running Kubernetes locally.

entries.title: Spec
entries.summary: Field in Kubernetes manifests that defines the desired state or configuration.

entries.title: Cluster Architect
entries.summary: A person who designs infrastructure that involves one or more Kubernetes clusters.

entries.title: Container Runtime
entries.summary: The container runtime is the software that is responsible for running containers.

entries.title: RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)
entries.summary: Manages authorization decisions, allowing admins to dynamically configure access policies through the Kubernetes API.

entries.title: Affinity
entries.summary: Rules used by the scheduler to determine where to place pods

entries.title: Pod Security Policy
entries.summary: Removed API that enforced Pod security restrictions.

entries.title: Pod Disruption Budget
entries.summary: An object that limits the number of Pods of a replicated application that are down simultaneously from voluntary disruptions.

entries.title: ReplicaSet
entries.summary: ReplicaSet ensures that a specified number of Pod replicas are running at one time

entries.title: Pod
entries.summary: A Pod represents a set of running containers in your cluster.

entries.title: Replica
entries.summary: Replicas are copies of pods, ensuring availability, scalability, and fault tolerance by maintaining identical instances.

entries.title: API resource
entries.summary: A Kubernetes entity, representing an endpoint on the Kubernetes API server.

entries.title: Member
entries.summary: A continuously active contributor in the K8s community.

entries.title: Cloud Controller Manager
entries.summary: Control plane component that integrates Kubernetes with third-party cloud providers.

entries.title: Docker
entries.summary: Docker is a software technology providing operating-system-level virtualization also known as containers.

entries.title: Workload
entries.summary: A workload is an application running on Kubernetes.

entries.title: Common Expression Language
entries.summary: An expression language that's designed to be safe for executing user code.

entries.title: Data Plane
entries.summary: The layer that provides capacity such as CPU, memory, network, and storage so that the containers can run and connect to a network.

entries.title: ServiceAccount
entries.summary: Provides an identity for processes that run in a Pod.

entries.title: Job
entries.summary: A finite or batch task that runs to completion.

entries.title: Group Version Resource
entries.summary: The API group, API version and name of a Kubernetes API.

entries.title: Proxy
entries.summary: An application acting as an intermediary between clients and servers

entries.title: kube-scheduler
entries.summary: Control plane component that watches for newly created pods with no assigned node, and selects a node for them to run on.

entries.title: Ingress
entries.summary: An API object that manages external access to the services in a cluster, typically HTTP.

entries.title: Approver
entries.summary: A person who can review and approve Kubernetes code contributions.

entries.title: Add-ons
entries.summary: Resources that extend the functionality of Kubernetes.

entries.title: Device
entries.summary: Any resource that's directly or indirectly attached your cluster's nodes, like GPUs or circuit boards.

entries.title: Container Lifecycle Hooks
entries.summary: The lifecycle hooks expose events in the container management lifecycle and let the user run code when the events occur.

entries.title: Mirror Pod
entries.summary: An object in the API server that tracks a static pod on a kubelet.

entries.title: App Container
entries.summary: A container used to run part of a workload. Compare with init container.

entries.title: Duration
entries.summary: A string value representing an amount of time.

entries.title: API Group
entries.summary: A set of related paths in the Kubernetes API.

entries.title: Selector
entries.summary: Allows users to filter a list of resources based on labels.

entries.title: CIDR
entries.summary: CIDR is a notation for describing blocks of IP addresses and is used heavily in various networking configurations.

entries.title: Container Runtime Interface (CRI)
entries.summary: Protocol for communication between the kubelet and the local container runtime.

entries.title: Container Storage Interface (CSI)
entries.summary: The Container Storage Interface (CSI) defines a standard interface to expose storage systems to containers.

entries.title: Container Device Interface (CDI)
entries.summary: A CNCF specification for describing device configuration that container runtimes apply when creating containers.

entries.title: Applications
entries.summary: The layer where various containerized applications run.

entries.title: Storage Class
entries.summary: A StorageClass provides a way for administrators to describe different available storage types.

entries.title: SIG (special interest group)
entries.summary: Community members who collectively manage an ongoing piece or aspect of the larger Kubernetes open source project.

entries.title: Persistent Volume
entries.summary: API object that represents a piece of storage in the cluster.

entries.title: Documentation
entries.summary: Documentation is available for Kubernetes.

entries.title: Available Documentation Versions
entries.summary: Multiple versions of documentation are available for Kubernetes.

entries.title: Kubernetes Documentation
entries.summary: Kubernetes is an open source container orchestration engine for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. The open source project is hosted by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation.

entries.title: Tasks
entries.summary: Tasks are a way to perform specific actions on your Kubernetes cluster, such as deploying applications or managing resources.

entries.title: Encodings
entries.summary: The process of converting data into a format that can be understood by different systems or applications.

entries.title: API Groups
entries.summary: Kubernetes API groups and their served versions.

entries.title: Kubernetes API
entries.summary: A set of APIs that provide programmatic access to Kubernetes resources and functionality.

entries.title: ClusterRoleBinding
entries.summary: ClusterRoleBinding references a ClusterRole, but not contain it.  It can reference a ClusterRole in the global namespace, and adds who information via Subject.

entries.title: RoleBinding
entries.summary: RoleBinding references a role, but does not contain it.  It can reference a Role in the same namespace or a ClusterRole in the global namespace. It adds who information via Subjects and namespace information by which namespace it exists in.  RoleBindings in a given namespace only have effect in that namespace.

entries.title: Role
entries.summary: Role is a namespaced, logical grouping of PolicyRules that can be referenced as a unit by a RoleBinding.

entries.title: ClusterRole
entries.summary: ClusterRole is a cluster level, logical grouping of PolicyRules that can be referenced as a unit by a RoleBinding or ClusterRoleBinding.

entries.title: Rbac
entries.summary: A mechanism for controlling access to Kubernetes resources based on user identity and role.

entries.title: StorageVersion
entries.summary: Storage version of a specific resource.

entries.title: Apiserverinternal
entries.summary: Apiserverinternal is an internal API of Kubernetes.

entries.title: FlowSchema
entries.summary: FlowSchema defines the schema of a group of flows. Note that a flow is made up of a set of inbound API requests with similar attributes and is identified by a pair of strings: the name of the FlowSchema and a "flow distinguisher".

entries.title: PriorityLevelConfiguration
entries.summary: PriorityLevelConfiguration represents the configuration of a priority level.

entries.title: Flowcontrol
entries.summary: Flowcontrol is an internal component of Kubernetes that manages API requests.

entries.title: Node
entries.summary: Node is a worker node in Kubernetes. Each node will have a unique identifier in the cache (i.e. in etcd).

entries.title: Namespace
entries.summary: Namespace provides a scope for Names. Use of multiple namespaces is optional.

entries.title: LimitRange
entries.summary: LimitRange sets resource usage limits for each kind of resource in a Namespace.

entries.title: ServiceAccount
entries.summary: ServiceAccount binds together: * a name, understood by users, and perhaps by peripheral systems, for an identity * a principal that can be authenticated and authorized * a set of secrets

entries.title: PodTemplate
entries.summary: PodTemplate describes a template for creating copies of a predefined pod.

entries.title: PersistentVolume
entries.summary: PersistentVolume (PV) is a storage resource provisioned by an administrator. It is analogous to a node. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/persistent-volumes

entries.title: ConfigMap
entries.summary: ConfigMap holds configuration data for pods to consume.

entries.title: Secret
entries.summary: Secret holds secret data of a certain type. The total bytes of the values in the Data field must be less than MaxSecretSize bytes.

entries.title: Pod
entries.summary: Pod is a collection of containers that can run on a host. This resource is created by clients and scheduled onto hosts.

entries.title: ReplicationController
entries.summary: ReplicationController represents the configuration of a replication controller.

entries.title: ResourceQuota
entries.summary: ResourceQuota sets aggregate quota restrictions enforced per namespace

entries.title: Endpoints
PersistentVolumeClaim
entries.summary: PersistentVolumeClaim is a user's request for and claim to a persistent volume

entries.title: Service
entries.summary: Service is a named abstraction of software service (for example, mysql) consisting of local port (for example 3306) that the proxy listens on, and the selector that determines which pods will answer requests sent through the proxy.

entries.title: Event
entries.summary: Event is a report of an event somewhere in the cluster.  Events have a limited retention time and triggers and messages may evolve with time.  Event consumers should not rely on the timing of an event with a given Reason reflecting a consistent underlying trigger, or the continued existence of events with that Reason.  Events should be treated as informative, best-effort, supplemental data.

entries.title: Core
entries.summary: Core is a set of APIs in Kubernetes that provide basic functionality.

entries.title: ComponentStatus
entries.summary: ComponentStatus (and ComponentStatusList) holds the cluster validation info. Deprecated: This API is deprecated in v1.19+

entries.title: LeaseCandidate
entries.summary: LeaseCandidate defines a candidate for a Lease object. Candidates are created such that coordinated leader election will pick the best leader from the list of candidates.

entries.title: Lease
entries.summary: Lease defines a lease concept.

entries.title: Coordination
entries.summary: Coordination is a component of Kubernetes that manages distributed systems.

entries.title: PodGroup
entries.summary: PodGroup represents a runtime instance of pods grouped together. PodGroups are created by workload controllers (Job, LWS, JobSet, etc...) from Workload.podGroupTemplates. PodGroup API enablement is toggled by the GenericWorkload feature gate.

entries.title: Workload
entries.summary: Workload allows for expressing scheduling constraints that should be used when managing the lifecycle of workloads from the scheduling perspective, including scheduling, preemption, eviction and other phases. Workload API enablement is toggled by the GenericWorkload feature gate.

entries.title: PriorityClass
entries.summary: PriorityClass defines mapping from a priority class name to the priority integer value. The value can be any valid integer.

entries.title: Scheduling
entries.summary: Scheduling is a feature in Kubernetes that manages the placement of pods on nodes within a cluster.

entries.title: NetworkPolicy
entries.summary: NetworkPolicy describes what network traffic is allowed for a set of Pods

entries.title: ServiceCIDR
entries.summary: ServiceCIDR defines a range of IP addresses using CIDR format (e.g. 192.168.0.0/24 or 2001:db2::/64). This range is used to allocate ClusterIPs to Service objects.

entries.title: IPAddress
entries.summary: IPAddress represents a single IP of a single IP Family. The object is designed to be used by APIs that operate on IP addresses. The object is used by the Service core API for allocation of IP addresses. An IP address can be represented in different formats, to guarantee the uniqueness of the IP, the name of the object is the IP address in canonical format, four decimal digits separated by dots suppressing leading zeros for IPv4 and the representation defined by RFC 5952 for IPv6. Valid: 192.168.1.5 or 2001:db8::1 or 2001:db8:aaaa:bbbb:cccc:dddd:eeee:1 Invalid: 10.01.2.3 or 2001:db8:0:0:0::1

entries.title: IngressClass
entries.summary: IngressClass represents the class of the Ingress, referenced by the Ingress Spec. The `ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class` annotation can be used to indicate that an IngressClass should be considered default. When a single IngressClass resource has this annotation set to true, new Ingress resources without a class specified will be assigned this default class.

entries.title: Networking
entries.summary: Networking is a feature in Kubernetes that provides connectivity between pods and services within a cluster.

entries.title: Ingress
entries.summary: Ingress is a collection of rules that allow inbound connections to reach the endpoints defined by a backend. An Ingress can be configured to give services externally-reachable urls, load balance traffic, terminate SSL, offer name based virtual hosting etc.

entries.title: EndpointSlice
entries.summary: EndpointSlice represents a set of service endpoints. Most EndpointSlices are created by the EndpointSlice controller to represent the Pods selected by Service objects. For a given service there may be multiple EndpointSlice objects which must be joined to produce the full set of endpoints; you can find all of the slices for a given service by listing EndpointSlices in the service's namespace whose `kubernetes.io/service-name` label contains the service's name.

entries.title: Discovery
entries.summary: Discovery is a feature in Kubernetes that enables pods and services to find each other within a cluster.

entries.title: PodCertificateRequest
entries.summary:
PodCertificateRequest encodes a pod requesting a certificate from a given signer.

Kubelets use this API to implement podCertificate projected volumes

entries.title: ClusterTrustBundle
entries.summary:
ClusterTrustBundle is a cluster-scoped container for X.509 trust anchors (root certificates).

ClusterTrustBundle objects are considered to be readable by any authenticated user in the cluster, because they can be mounted by pods using the `clusterTrustBundle` projection.  All service accounts have read access to ClusterTrustBundles by default.  Users who only have namespace-level access to a cluster can read ClusterTrustBundles by impersonating a serviceaccount that they have access to.

It can be optionally associated with a particular assigner, in which case it contains one valid set of trust anchors for that signer. Signers may have multiple associated ClusterTrustBundles; each is an independent set of trust anchors for that signer. Admission control is used to enforce that only users with permissions on the signer can create or modify the corresponding bundle.

entries.title: CertificateSigningRequest
entries.summary:
CertificateSigningRequest objects provide a mechanism to obtain x509 certificates by submitting a certificate signing request, and having it asynchronously approved and issued.

Kubelets use this API to obtain:
 1. client certificates to authenticate to kube-apiserver (with the "kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client-kubelet" signerName).
 2. serving certificates for TLS endpoints kube-apiserver can connect to securely (with the "kubernetes.io/kubelet-serving" signerName).

This API can be used to request client certificates to authenticate to kube-apiserver (with the "kubernetes.io/kube-apiserver-client" signerName), or to obtain certificates from custom non-Kubernetes signers.

entries.title: Certificates
entries.summary: Certificates are a feature in Kubernetes that enables secure communication between pods and services within a cluster.

entries.title: CSIStorageCapacity
entries.summary:
CSIStorageCapacity stores the result of one CSI GetCapacity call. For a given StorageClass, this describes the available capacity in a particular topology segment.  This can be used when considering where to instantiate new PersistentVolumes.

For example this can express things like: - StorageClass "standard" has "1234 GiB" available in "topology.kubernetes.io/zone=us-east1" - StorageClass "localssd" has "10 GiB" available in "kubernetes.io/hostname=knode-abc123"

The following three cases all imply that no capacity is available for a certain combination: - no object exists with suitable topology and storage class name - such an object exists, but the capacity is unset - such an object exists, but the capacity is zero

The producer of these objects can decide which approach is more suitable.

They are consumed by the kube-scheduler when a CSI driver opts into capacity-aware scheduling with CSIDriverSpec.StorageCapacity. The scheduler compares the MaximumVolumeSize against the requested size of pending volumes to filter out unsuitable nodes. If MaximumVolumeSize is unset, it falls back to a comparison against the less precise Capacity. If that is also unset, the scheduler assumes that capacity is insufficient and tries some other node.

entries.title: VolumeAttributesClass
entries.summary: VolumeAttributesClass represents a specification of mutable volume attributes defined by the CSI driver. The class can be specified during dynamic provisioning of PersistentVolumeClaims, and changed in the PersistentVolumeClaim spec after provisioning.

entries.title: CSINode
entries.summary: CSINode holds information about all CSI drivers installed on a node. CSI drivers do not need to create the CSINode object directly. As long as they use the node-driver-registrar sidecar container, the kubelet will automatically populate the CSINode object for the CSI driver as part of kubelet plugin registration. CSINode has the same name as a node. If the object is missing, it means either there are no CSI Drivers available on the node, or the Kubelet version is low enough that it doesn't create this object. CSINode has an OwnerReference that points to the corresponding node object.

entries.title: StorageClass
entries.summary:
StorageClass describes the parameters for a class of storage for which PersistentVolumes can be dynamically provisioned.

StorageClasses are non-namespaced; the name of the storage class according to etcd is in ObjectMeta.Name.

entries.title: VolumeAttachment
entries.summary:
VolumeAttachment captures the intent to attach or detach the specified volume to/from the specified node.

VolumeAttachment objects are non-namespaced.

entries.title: Storage
entries.summary: Storage in Kubernetes refers to the management and organization of data storage resources.

entries.title: CSIDriver
entries.summary: CSIDriver captures information about a Container Storage Interface (CSI) volume driver deployed on the cluster. Kubernetes attach detach controller uses this object to determine whether attach is required. Kubelet uses this object to determine whether pod information needs to be passed on mount. CSIDriver objects are non-namespaced.

entries.title: Job
entries.summary: Job represents the configuration of a single job.

entries.title: CronJob
entries.summary: CronJob represents the configuration of a single cron job.

entries.title: Batch
entries.summary: Batch in Kubernetes refers to the execution of batch jobs, which are applications that run to completion.

entries.title: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration
entries.summary: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration describes the configuration of and admission webhook that accept or reject and object without changing it.

entries.title: MutatingAdmissionPolicy
entries.summary: MutatingAdmissionPolicy describes the definition of an admission mutation policy that mutates the object coming into admission chain.

entries.title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy
entries.summary: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy describes the definition of an admission validation policy that accepts or rejects an object without changing it.

entries.title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding
entries.summary:
MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding binds the MutatingAdmissionPolicy with parametrized resources. MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding and the optional parameter resource together define how cluster administrators configure policies for clusters.

For a given admission request, each binding will cause its policy to be evaluated N times, where N is 1 for policies/bindings that don't use params, otherwise N is the number of parameters selected by the binding. Each evaluation is constrained by a [runtime cost budget](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/using-api/cel/#runtime-cost-budget).

Adding/removing policies, bindings, or params can not affect whether a given (policy, binding, param) combination is within its own CEL budget.

entries.title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding
entries.summary:
ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding binds the ValidatingAdmissionPolicy with paramerized resources. ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding and parameter CRDs together define how cluster administrators configure policies for clusters.

For a given admission request, each binding will cause its policy to be evaluated N times, where N is 1 for policies/bindings that don't use params, otherwise N is the number of parameters selected by the binding.

The CEL expressions of a policy must have a computed CEL cost below the maximum CEL budget. Each evaluation of the policy is given an independent CEL cost budget. Adding/removing policies, bindings, or params can not affect whether a given (policy, binding, param) combination is within its own CEL budget.

entries.title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration
entries.summary: MutatingWebhookConfiguration describes the configuration of and admission webhook that accept or reject and may change the object.

entries.title: Admissionregistration
entries.summary: Admissionregistration in Kubernetes refers to the process of validating and mutating API requests before they are processed.

entries.title: APIService
entries.summary: APIService represents a server for a particular GroupVersion. Name must be "version.group".

entries.title: Apiregistration
entries.summary: Apiregistration in Kubernetes refers to the process of registering and managing API servers.

entries.title: RuntimeClass
entries.summary: RuntimeClass defines a class of container runtime supported in the cluster. The RuntimeClass is used to determine which container runtime is used to run all containers in a pod. RuntimeClasses are manually defined by a user or cluster provisioner, and referenced in the PodSpec. The Kubelet is responsible for resolving the RuntimeClassName reference before running the pod.  For more details, see https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/containers/runtime-class/

entries.title: Node
entries.summary: A Node is a worker machine in Kubernetes, where containers are run.

entries.title: Event
entries.summary: Event is a report of an event somewhere in the cluster. It generally denotes some state change in the system. Events have a limited retention time and triggers and messages may evolve with time.  Event consumers should not rely on the timing of an event with a given Reason reflecting a consistent underlying trigger, or the continued existence of events with that Reason.  Events should be treated as informative, best-effort, supplemental data.

entries.title: Events
entries.summary: Events are a way for Kubernetes to report on the status of resources.

entries.title: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
entries.summary: HorizontalPodAutoscaler is the configuration for a horizontal pod autoscaler, which automatically manages the replica count of any resource implementing the scale subresource based on the metrics specified.

entries.title: Autoscaling
entries.summary: Autoscaling allows Kubernetes to automatically adjust the number of replicas for a deployment.

entries.title: ResourcePoolStatusRequest
entries.summary: ResourcePoolStatusRequest triggers a one-time calculation of resource pool status based on the provided filters. Once status is set, the request is considered complete and will not be reprocessed. Users should delete and recreate requests to get updated information.

entries.title: ResourceSlice
entries.summary:
ResourceSlice represents one or more resources in a pool of similar resources, managed by a common driver. A pool may span more than one ResourceSlice, and exactly how many ResourceSlices comprise a pool is determined by the driver.

At the moment, the only supported resources are devices with attributes and capacities. Each device in a given pool, regardless of how many ResourceSlices, must have a unique name. The ResourceSlice in which a device gets published may change over time. The unique identifier for a device is the tuple <driver name>, <pool name>, <device name>.

Whenever a driver needs to update a pool, it increments the pool.Spec.Pool.Generation number and updates all ResourceSlices with that new number and new resource definitions. A consumer must only use ResourceSlices with the highest generation number and ignore all others.

When allocating all resources in a pool matching certain criteria or when looking for the best solution among several different alternatives, a consumer should check the number of ResourceSlices in a pool (included in each ResourceSlice) to determine whether its view of a pool is complete and if not, should wait until the driver has completed updating the pool.

For resources that are not local to a node, the node name is not set. Instead, the driver may use a node selector to specify where the devices are available.

entries.title: DeviceClass
entries.summary: DeviceClass is a vendor- or admin-provided resource that contains device configuration and selectors. It can be referenced in the device requests of a claim to apply these presets. Cluster scoped.

entries.title: ResourceClaimTemplate
entries.summary: ResourceClaimTemplate is used to produce ResourceClaim objects.

entries.title: ResourceClaim
entries.summary: ResourceClaim describes a request for access to resources in the cluster, for use by workloads. For example, if a workload needs an accelerator device with specific properties, this is how that request is expressed. The status stanza tracks whether this claim has been satisfied and what specific resources have been allocated.

entries.title: Resource
entries.summary: Resources are the units of compute, memory, and storage that pods require.

entries.title: DeviceTaintRule
entries.summary: DeviceTaintRule adds one taint to all devices which match the selector. This has the same effect as if the taint was specified directly in the ResourceSlice by the DRA driver.

entries.title: FieldSelectorRequirement
entries.summary: FieldSelectorRequirement is a selector that contains values, a key, and an operator that relates the key and values.

entries.title: Subject
entries.summary: Subject contains a reference to the object or user identities a role binding applies to.  This can either hold a direct API object reference, or a value for non-objects such as user and group names.

entries.title: GroupVersionForDiscovery
entries.summary: GroupVersion contains the "group/version" and "version" string of a version. It is made a struct to keep extensibility.

entries.title: TypedLocalObjectReference
entries.summary: TypedLocalObjectReference contains enough information to let you locate the typed referenced object inside the same namespace.

entries.title: Status
entries.summary: Status is a return value for calls that don't return other objects.

entries.title: ParamRef
entries.summary: ParamRef describes how to locate the params to be used as input to expressions of rules applied by a policy binding.

entries.title: NonResourceRule
entries.summary: NonResourceRule holds information that describes a rule for the non-resource

entries.title: UserInfo
entries.summary: UserInfo holds the information about the user needed to implement the user.Info interface.

entries.title: ResourceRule
entries.summary: ResourceRule is the list of actions the subject is allowed to perform on resources. The list ordering isn't significant, may contain duplicates, and possibly be incomplete.

entries.title: Toleration
entries.summary: The pod this Toleration is attached to tolerates any taint that matches the triple <key,value,effect> using the matching operator <operator>.

entries.title: RoleRef
entries.summary: RoleRef contains information that points to the role being used

entries.title: LocalSubjectAccessReview
entries.summary: LocalSubjectAccessReview checks whether or not a user or group can perform an action in a given namespace. Having a namespace scoped resource makes it much easier to grant namespace scoped policy that includes permissions checking.

entries.title: LabelSelectorAttributes
entries.summary: LabelSelectorAttributes indicates a label limited access. Webhook authors are encouraged to * ensure rawSelector and requirements are not both set * consider the requirements field if set * not try to parse or consider the rawSelector field if set. This is to avoid another CVE-2022-2880 (i.e. getting different systems to agree on how exactly to parse a query is not something we want), see https://www.oxeye.io/resources/golang-parameter-smuggling-attack for more details. For the *SubjectAccessReview endpoints of the kube-apiserver: * If rawSelector is empty and requirements are empty, the request is not limited. * If rawSelector is present and requirements are empty, the rawSelector will be parsed and limited if the parsing succeeds. * If rawSelector is empty and requirements are present, the requirements should be honored * If rawSelector is present and requirements are present, the request is invalid.

entries.title: EventSource
entries.summary: EventSource contains information for an event.

entries.title: APIResource
entries.summary: APIResource specifies the name of a resource and whether it is namespaced.

entries.title: ResourceAttributes
entries.summary: ResourceAttributes includes the authorization attributes available for resource requests to the Authorizer interface

entries.title: StatusCause
entries.summary: StatusCause provides more information about an api.Status failure, including cases when multiple errors are encountered.

entries.title: Condition
entries.summary: Condition contains details for one aspect of the current state of this API Resource.

entries.title: MatchCondition
entries.summary: MatchCondition represents a condition which must by fulfilled for a request to be sent to a webhook.

entries.title: Patch
entries.summary: Patch is provided to give a concrete name and type to the Kubernetes PATCH request body.

entries.title: Eviction
entries.summary: Eviction evicts a pod from its node subject to certain policies and safety constraints. This is a subresource of Pod.  A request to cause such an eviction is created by POSTing to .../pods/<pod name>/evictions.

entries.title: NodeSelector
entries.summary: A node selector represents the union of the results of one or more label queries over a set of nodes; that is, it represents the OR of the selectors represented by the node selector terms.

entries.title: Quantity
entries.summary:
Quantity is a fixed-point representation of a number. It provides convenient marshaling/unmarshaling in JSON and YAML, in addition to String() and AsInt64() accessors.

The serialization format is:

``` <quantity>        ::= <signedNumber><suffix>

	(Note that <suffix> may be empty, from the "" case in <decimalSI>.)

<digit>           ::= 0 | 1 | ... | 9 <digits>          ::= <digit> | <digit><digits> <number>          ::= <digits> | <digits>.<digits> | <digits>. | .<digits> <sign>            ::= "+" | "-" <signedNumber>    ::= <number> | <sign><number> <suffix>          ::= <binarySI> | <decimalExponent> | <decimalSI> <binarySI>        ::= Ki | Mi | Gi | Ti | Pi | Ei

	(International System of units; See: http://physics.nist.gov/cuu/Units/binary.html)

<decimalSI>       ::= m | "" | k | M | G | T | P | E

	(Note that 1024 = 1Ki but 1000 = 1k; I didn't choose the capitalization.)

<decimalExponent> ::= "e" <signedNumber> | "E" <signedNumber> ```

No matter which of the three exponent forms is used, no quantity may represent a number greater than 2^63-1 in magnitude, nor may it have more than 3 decimal places. Numbers larger or more precise will be capped or rounded up. (E.g.: 0.1m will rounded up to 1m.) This may be extended in the future if we require larger or smaller quantities.

When a Quantity is parsed from a string, it will remember the type of suffix it had, and will use the same type again when it is serialized.

Before serializing, Quantity will be put in "canonical form". This means that Exponent/suffix will be adjusted up or down (with a corresponding increase or decrease in Mantissa) such that:

- No precision is lost - No fractional digits will be emitted - The exponent (or suffix) is as large as possible.

The sign will be omitted unless the number is negative.

Examples:

- 1.5 will be serialized as "1500m" - 1.5Gi will be serialized as "1536Mi"

Note that the quantity will NEVER be internally represented by a floating point number. That is the whole point of this exercise.

Non-canonical values will still parse as long as they are well formed, but will be re-emitted in their canonical form. (So always use canonical form, or don't diff.)

This format is intended to make it difficult to use these numbers without writing some sort of special handling code in the hopes that that will cause implementors to also use a fixed point implementation.

entries.title: BasicDevice
entries.summary: BasicDevice defines one device instance.

entries.title: Preconditions
entries.summary: Preconditions must be fulfilled before an operation (update, delete, etc.) is carried out.

entries.title: SelfSubjectRulesReview
entries.summary: SelfSubjectRulesReview enumerates the set of actions the current user can perform within a namespace. The returned list of actions may be incomplete depending on the server's authorization mode, and any errors experienced during the evaluation. SelfSubjectRulesReview should be used by UIs to show/hide actions, or to quickly let an end user reason about their permissions. It should NOT Be used by external systems to drive authorization decisions as this raises confused deputy, cache lifetime/revocation, and correctness concerns. SubjectAccessReview, and LocalAccessReview are the correct way to defer authorization decisions to the API server.

entries.title: NamedRuleWithOperations
entries.summary: NamedRuleWithOperations is a tuple of Operations and Resources with ResourceNames.

entries.title: RuleWithOperations
entries.summary: RuleWithOperations is a tuple of Operations and Resources. It is recommended to make sure that all the tuple expansions are valid.

entries.title: NodeSelectorTerm
entries.summary: A null or empty node selector term matches no objects. The requirements of them are ANDed. The TopologySelectorTerm type implements a subset of the NodeSelectorTerm.

entries.title: ObjectReference
entries.summary: ObjectReference contains enough information to let you inspect or modify the referred object.

entries.title: FieldSelectorAttributes
entries.summary: FieldSelectorAttributes indicates a field limited access. Webhook authors are encouraged to * ensure rawSelector and requirements are not both set * consider the requirements field if set * not try to parse or consider the rawSelector field if set. This is to avoid another CVE-2022-2880 (i.e. getting different systems to agree on how exactly to parse a query is not something we want), see https://www.oxeye.io/resources/golang-parameter-smuggling-attack for more details. For the *SubjectAccessReview endpoints of the kube-apiserver: * If rawSelector is empty and requirements are empty, the request is not limited. * If rawSelector is present and requirements are empty, the rawSelector will be parsed and limited if the parsing succeeds. * If rawSelector is empty and requirements are present, the requirements should be honored * If rawSelector is present and requirements are present, the request is invalid.

entries.title: SubjectAccessReview
entries.summary: SubjectAccessReview checks whether or not a user or group can perform an action.

entries.title: PolicyRule
entries.summary: PolicyRule holds information that describes a policy rule, but does not contain information about who the rule applies to or which namespace the rule applies to.

entries.title: NonResourceAttributes
entries.summary: NonResourceAttributes includes the authorization attributes available for non-resource requests to the Authorizer interface

entries.title: ManagedFieldsEntry
entries.summary: ManagedFieldsEntry is a workflow-id, a FieldSet and the group version of the resource that the fieldset applies to.

entries.title: ParamKind
entries.summary: ParamKind is a tuple of Group Kind and Version.

entries.title: ServerAddressByClientCIDR
entries.summary: ServerAddressByClientCIDR helps the client to determine the server address that they should use, depending on the clientCIDR that they match.

entries.title: GroupResource
entries.summary: GroupResource specifies a Group and a Resource, but does not force a version.  This is useful for identifying concepts during lookup stages without having partially valid types

entries.title: Scale
entries.summary: Scale represents a scaling request for a resource.

entries.title: DeleteOptions
entries.summary: DeleteOptions may be provided when deleting an API object.

entries.title: LabelSelectorRequirement
entries.summary: A label selector requirement is a selector that contains values, a key, and an operator that relates the key and values.

entries.title: LocalObjectReference
entries.summary: LocalObjectReference contains enough information to let you locate the referenced object inside the same namespace.

entries.title: OwnerReference
entries.summary: OwnerReference contains enough information to let you identify an owning object. An owning object must be in the same namespace as the dependent, or be cluster-scoped, so there is no namespace field.

entries.title: Time
entries.summary: Time is a wrapper around time.Time which supports correct marshaling to YAML and JSON.  Wrappers are provided for many of the factory methods that the time package offers.

entries.title: MicroTime
entries.summary: MicroTime is version of Time with microsecond level precision.

entries.title: WebhookClientConfig
entries.summary: WebhookClientConfig contains the information to make a TLS connection with the webhook

entries.title: TokenReview
entries.summary: TokenReview attempts to authenticate a token to a known user. Note: TokenReview requests may be cached by the webhook token authenticator plugin in the kube-apiserver.

entries.title: WatchEvent
entries.summary: Event represents a single event to a watched resource.

entries.title: APIGroup
entries.summary: APIGroup contains the name, the supported versions, and the preferred version of a group.

entries.title: ServiceReference
entries.summary: ServiceReference holds a reference to Service.legacy.k8s.io

entries.title: FieldsV1
entries.summary:
FieldsV1 stores a set of fields in a data structure like a Trie, in JSON format.

Each key is either a '.' representing the field itself, and will always map to an empty set, or a string representing a sub-field or item. The string will follow one of these four formats: 'f:<name>', where <name> is the name of a field in a struct, or key in a map 'v:<value>', where <value> is the exact json formatted value of a list item 'i:<index>', where <index> is position of a item in a list 'k:<keys>', where <keys> is a map of  a list item's key fields to their unique values If a key maps to an empty Fields value, the field that key represents is part of the set.

The exact format is defined in sigs.k8s.io/structured-merge-diff

entries.title: ObjectMeta
entries.summary: ObjectMeta is metadata that all persisted resources must have, which includes all objects users must create.

entries.title: Binding
entries.summary: Binding ties one object to another; for example, a pod is bound to a node by a scheduler.

entries.title: ListMeta
entries.summary: ListMeta describes metadata that synthetic resources must have, including lists and various status objects. A resource may have only one of {ObjectMeta, ListMeta}.

entries.title: LabelSelector
entries.summary: A label selector is a label query over a set of resources. The result of matchLabels and matchExpressions are ANDed. An empty label selector matches all objects. A null label selector matches no objects.

entries.title: SelfSubjectReview
entries.summary: SelfSubjectReview contains the user information that the kube-apiserver has about the user making this request. When using impersonation, users will receive the user info of the user being impersonated.  If impersonation or request header authentication is used, any extra keys will have their case ignored and returned as lowercase.

entries.title: SelfSubjectAccessReview
entries.summary: SelfSubjectAccessReview checks whether or the current user can perform an action.  Not filling in a spec.namespace means "in all namespaces".  Self is a special case, because users should always be able to check whether they can perform an action

entries.title: Variable
entries.summary: Variable is the definition of a variable that is used for composition. A variable is defined as a named expression.

entries.title: APIVersions
entries.summary: APIVersions lists the versions that are available, to allow clients to discover the API at /api, which is the root path of the legacy v1 API.

entries.title: TypedLocalObjectReference
entries.summary: TypedLocalObjectReference allows to reference typed object inside the same namespace.

entries.title: SubjectRulesReviewStatus
entries.summary: SubjectRulesReviewStatus contains the result of a rules check. This check can be incomplete depending on the set of authorizers the server is configured with and any errors experienced during evaluation. Because authorization rules are additive, if a rule appears in a list it's safe to assume the subject has that permission, even if that list is incomplete.

entries.title: StatusDetails
entries.summary: StatusDetails is a set of additional properties that MAY be set by the server to provide additional information about a response. The Reason field of a Status object defines what attributes will be set. Clients must ignore fields that do not match the defined type of each attribute, and should assume that any attribute may be empty, invalid, or under defined.

entries.title: Definitions
entries.summary: Definitions is a collection of Kubernetes API object definitions.

entries.title: ShardInfo
entries.summary: ShardInfo describes the shard selector that was applied to produce a list response. Its presence on a list response indicates the list is a filtered subset.

entries.title: MatchResources
entries.summary: MatchResources decides whether to run the admission control policy on an object based on whether it meets the match criteria. The exclude rules take precedence over include rules (if a resource matches both, it is excluded)

entries.title: StorageVersionMigration
entries.summary: StorageVersionMigration represents a migration of stored data to the latest storage version.

entries.title: Storagemigration
entries.summary: Storagemigration is a Kubernetes API object that represents storage migration.

entries.title: DaemonSet
entries.summary: DaemonSet represents the configuration of a daemon set.

entries.title: ReplicaSet
entries.summary: ReplicaSet ensures that a specified number of pod replicas are running at any given time.

entries.title: StatefulSet
entries.summary:
StatefulSet represents a set of pods with consistent identities. Identities are defined as:
  - Network: A single stable DNS and hostname.
  - Storage: As many VolumeClaims as requested.

The StatefulSet guarantees that a given network identity will always map to the same storage identity.

entries.title: ControllerRevision
entries.summary: ControllerRevision implements an immutable snapshot of state data. Clients are responsible for serializing and deserializing the objects that contain their internal state. Once a ControllerRevision has been successfully created, it can not be updated. The API Server will fail validation of all requests that attempt to mutate the Data field. ControllerRevisions may, however, be deleted. Note that, due to its use by both the DaemonSet and StatefulSet controllers for update and rollback, this object is beta. However, it may be subject to name and representation changes in future releases, and clients should not depend on its stability. It is primarily for internal use by controllers.

entries.title: Deployment
entries.summary: Deployment enables declarative updates for Pods and ReplicaSets.

entries.title: Apps
entries.summary: Apps is a collection of Kubernetes API objects related to application management.

entries.title: CustomResourceDefinition
entries.summary: CustomResourceDefinition represents a resource that should be exposed on the API server.  Its name MUST be in the format <.spec.name>.<.spec.group>.

entries.title: Apiextensions
entries.summary: Apiextensions is a Kubernetes API that allows users to manage and extend the Kubernetes cluster's configuration.

entries.title: PodDisruptionBudget
entries.summary: PodDisruptionBudget is an object to define the max disruption that can be caused to a collection of pods

entries.title: Policy
entries.summary: Policy is a Kubernetes API that provides features for managing and enforcing cluster-wide policies.

entries.title: WatchList
entries.summary: A feature gate that enables or disables the WatchList API in Kubernetes.

entries.title: CSIMigrationAzureFile

entries.title: AdmissionWebhookMatchConditions

entries.title: ListFromCacheSnapshot

entries.title: TTLAfterFinished

entries.title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding
entries.summary:
MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding binds the MutatingAdmissionPolicy with parametrized resources. MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding and the optional parameter resource together define how cluster administrators configure policies for clusters.

For a given admission request, each binding will cause its policy to be evaluated N times, where N is 1 for policies/bindings that don't use params, otherwise N is the number of parameters selected by the binding. Each evaluation is constrained by a [runtime cost budget](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/using-api/cel/#runtime-cost-budget).

Adding/removing policies, bindings, or params can not affect whether a given (policy, binding, param) combination is within its own CEL budget.

entries.title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBindingList
entries.summary: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBindingList is a list of MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding.

entries.title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBindingSpec
entries.summary: MutatingAdmissionPolicyBindingSpec defines the specification of the MutatingAdmissionPolicyBinding.

entries.title: MutatingAdmissionPolicyList
entries.summary: MutatingAdmissionPolicyList is a list of MutatingAdmissionPolicy.

entries.title: MutatingAdmissionPolicySpec
entries.summary: MutatingAdmissionPolicySpec defines the desired behavior of the admission policy.

entries.title: MutatingWebhook
entries.summary: MutatingWebhook describes an admission webhook and the resources and operations it applies to.

entries.title: MutatingWebhookConfiguration
entries.summary: MutatingWebhookConfiguration describes the configuration of and admission webhook that accept or reject and may change the object.

entries.title: MutatingWebhookConfigurationList
entries.summary: MutatingWebhookConfigurationList is a list of MutatingWebhookConfiguration.

entries.title: Mutation
entries.summary: Mutation specifies the CEL expression which is used to apply the Mutation.

entries.title: NamedRuleWithOperations
entries.summary: NamedRuleWithOperations is a tuple of Operations and Resources with ResourceNames.

entries.title: ParamKind
entries.summary: ParamKind is a tuple of Group Kind and Version.

entries.title: ParamRef
entries.summary: ParamRef describes how to locate the params to be used as input to expressions of rules applied by a policy binding.

entries.title: RuleWithOperations
entries.summary: RuleWithOperations is a tuple of Operations and Resources. It is recommended to make sure that all the tuple expansions are valid.

entries.title: ServiceReference
entries.summary: ServiceReference holds a reference to Service.legacy.k8s.io

entries.title: TypeChecking
entries.summary: TypeChecking contains results of type checking the expressions in the ValidatingAdmissionPolicy

entries.title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy
entries.summary: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy describes the definition of an admission validation policy that accepts or rejects an object without changing it.

entries.title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding
entries.summary:
ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding binds the ValidatingAdmissionPolicy with paramerized resources. ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding and parameter CRDs together define how cluster administrators configure policies for clusters.

For a given admission request, each binding will cause its policy to be evaluated N times, where N is 1 for policies/bindings that don't use params, otherwise N is the number of parameters selected by the binding.

The CEL expressions of a policy must have a computed CEL cost below the maximum CEL budget. Each evaluation of the policy is given an independent CEL cost budget. Adding/removing policies, bindings, or params can not affect whether a given (policy, binding, param) combination is within its own CEL budget.

entries.title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBindingList
entries.summary: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBindingList is a list of ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding.

entries.title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBindingSpec
entries.summary: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBindingSpec is the specification of the ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding.

entries.title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyList
entries.summary: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyList is a list of ValidatingAdmissionPolicy.

entries.title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicySpec
entries.summary: ValidatingAdmissionPolicySpec is the specification of the desired behavior of the AdmissionPolicy.

entries.title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyStatus
entries.summary: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyStatus represents the status of an admission validation policy.

entries.title: ValidatingWebhook
entries.summary: ValidatingWebhook describes an admission webhook and the resources and operations it applies to.

entries.title: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration
entries.summary: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration describes the configuration of and admission webhook that accept or reject and object without changing it.

entries.title: ValidatingWebhookConfigurationList
entries.summary: ValidatingWebhookConfigurationList is a list of ValidatingWebhookConfiguration.

entries.title: Validation
entries.summary: Validation specifies the CEL expression which is used to apply the validation.

entries.title: Variable
entries.summary: Variable is the definition of a variable that is used for composition. A variable is defined as a named expression.

entries.title: WebhookClientConfig
entries.summary: WebhookClientConfig contains the information to make a TLS connection with the webhook

entries.title: ServerStorageVersion
entries.summary: An API server instance reports the version it can decode and the version it encodes objects to when persisting objects in the backend.

entries.title: StorageVersion
entries.summary: A StorageVersion is an object that represents the version of a storage system in Kubernetes.

entries.title: StorageVersionCondition
entries.summary: Describes the state of the storageVersion at a certain point.

entries.title: StorageVersionList
entries.summary: A list of storage versions.

entries.title: StorageVersionSpec
entries.summary: Describes a storage version.

entries.title: StorageVersionStatus
entries.summary: API server instances report the versions they can decode and the version they encode objects to when persisting objects in the backend.

entries.title: ControllerRevision
entries.summary: ControllerRevision implements an immutable snapshot of state data. Clients are responsible for serializing and deserializing the objects that contain their internal state. Once a ControllerRevision has been successfully created, it can not be updated. The API Server will fail validation of all requests that attempt to mutate the Data field. ControllerRevisions may, however, be deleted. Note that, due to its use by both the DaemonSet and StatefulSet controllers for update and rollback, this object is beta. However, it may be subject to name and representation changes in future releases, and clients should not depend on its stability. It is primarily for internal use by controllers.

entries.title: ControllerRevisionList
entries.summary: ControllerRevisionList is a resource containing a list of ControllerRevision objects.

entries.title: DaemonSet
entries.summary: DaemonSet represents the configuration of a daemon set.

entries.title: DaemonSetCondition
entries.summary: DaemonSetCondition describes the state of a DaemonSet at a certain point.

entries.title: DaemonSetList
entries.summary: DaemonSetList is a collection of daemon sets.

entries.title: DaemonSetSpec
entries.summary: DaemonSetSpec is the specification of a daemon set.

entries.title: DaemonSetStatus
entries.summary: DaemonSetStatus represents the current status of a daemon set.

entries.title: DaemonSetUpdateStrategy
entries.summary: DaemonSetUpdateStrategy is a struct used to control the update strategy for a DaemonSet.

entries.title: Deployment
entries.summary: Deployment enables declarative updates for Pods and ReplicaSets.

entries.title: DeploymentCondition
entries.summary: DeploymentCondition describes the state of a deployment at a certain point.

entries.title: DeploymentList
entries.summary: DeploymentList is a list of Deployments.

entries.title: DeploymentSpec
entries.summary: DeploymentSpec is the specification of the desired behavior of the Deployment.

entries.title: DeploymentStatus
entries.summary: DeploymentStatus is the most recently observed status of the Deployment.

entries.title: DeploymentStrategy
entries.summary: DeploymentStrategy describes how to replace existing pods with new ones.

entries.title: ReplicaSet
entries.summary: ReplicaSet ensures that a specified number of pod replicas are running at any given time.

entries.title: ReplicaSetCondition
entries.summary: ReplicaSetCondition describes the state of a replica set at a certain point.

entries.title: ReplicaSetList
entries.summary: ReplicaSetList is a collection of ReplicaSets.

entries.title: ReplicaSetSpec
entries.summary: ReplicaSetSpec is the specification of a ReplicaSet.

entries.title: ReplicaSetStatus
entries.summary: ReplicaSetStatus represents the current status of a ReplicaSet.

entries.title: RollingUpdateDaemonSet
entries.summary: Spec to control the desired behavior of daemon set rolling update.

entries.title: RollingUpdateDeployment
entries.summary: Spec to control the desired behavior of rolling update.

entries.title: RollingUpdateStatefulSetStrategy
entries.summary: RollingUpdateStatefulSetStrategy is used to communicate parameter for RollingUpdateStatefulSetStrategyType.

entries.title: StatefulSet
entries.summary:
StatefulSet represents a set of pods with consistent identities. Identities are defined as:
  - Network: A single stable DNS and hostname.
  - Storage: As many VolumeClaims as requested.

The StatefulSet guarantees that a given network identity will always map to the same storage identity.

entries.title: StatefulSetCondition
entries.summary: StatefulSetCondition describes the state of a statefulset at a certain point.

entries.title: StatefulSetList
entries.summary: StatefulSetList is a collection of StatefulSets.

entries.title: StatefulSetOrdinals
entries.summary: StatefulSetOrdinals describes the policy used for replica ordinal assignment in this StatefulSet.

entries.title: StatefulSetPersistentVolumeClaimRetentionPolicy
entries.summary: StatefulSetPersistentVolumeClaimRetentionPolicy describes the policy used for PVCs created from the StatefulSet VolumeClaimTemplates.

entries.title: StatefulSetSpec
entries.summary: A StatefulSetSpec is the specification of a StatefulSet.

entries.title: StatefulSetStatus
entries.summary: StatefulSetStatus represents the current state of a StatefulSet.

entries.title: StatefulSetUpdateStrategy
entries.summary: StatefulSetUpdateStrategy indicates the strategy that the StatefulSet controller will use to perform updates. It includes any additional parameters necessary to perform the update for the indicated strategy.

entries.title: BoundObjectReference
entries.summary: BoundObjectReference is a reference to an object that a token is bound to.

entries.title: SelfSubjectReview
entries.summary: SelfSubjectReview contains the user information that the kube-apiserver has about the user making this request. When using impersonation, users will receive the user info of the user being impersonated.  If impersonation or request header authentication is used, any extra keys will have their case ignored and returned as lowercase.

entries.title: SelfSubjectReviewStatus
entries.summary: SelfSubjectReviewStatus is filled by the kube-apiserver and sent back to a user.

entries.title: TokenRequest
entries.summary: TokenRequest requests a token for a given service account.

entries.title: TokenRequestSpec
entries.summary: TokenRequestSpec contains client provided parameters of a token request.

entries.title: TokenRequestStatus
entries.summary: TokenRequestStatus is the result of a token request.

entries.title: TokenReview
entries.summary: TokenReview attempts to authenticate a token to a known user. Note: TokenReview requests may be cached by the webhook token authenticator plugin in the kube-apiserver.

entries.title: TokenReviewSpec
entries.summary: TokenReviewSpec is a description of the token authentication request.

entries.title: TokenReviewStatus
entries.summary: TokenReviewStatus is the result of the token authentication request.

entries.title: UserInfo
entries.summary: UserInfo holds the information about the user needed to implement the user.Info interface.

entries.title: FieldSelectorAttributes
entries.summary: FieldSelectorAttributes indicates a field limited access. Webhook authors are encouraged to * ensure rawSelector and requirements are not both set * consider the requirements field if set * not try to parse or consider the rawSelector field if set. This is to avoid another CVE-2022-2880 (i.e. getting different systems to agree on how exactly to parse a query is not something we want), see https://www.oxeye.io/resources/golang-parameter-smuggling-attack for more details. For the *SubjectAccessReview endpoints of the kube-apiserver: * If rawSelector is empty and requirements are empty, the request is not limited. * If rawSelector is present and requirements are empty, the rawSelector will be parsed and limited if the parsing succeeds. * If rawSelector is empty and requirements are present, the requirements should be honored * If rawSelector is present and requirements are present, the request is invalid.

entries.title: LabelSelectorAttributes
entries.summary: LabelSelectorAttributes indicates a label limited access. Webhook authors are encouraged to * ensure rawSelector and requirements are not both set * consider the requirements field if set * not try to parse or consider the rawSelector field if set. This is to avoid another CVE-2022-2880 (i.e. getting different systems to agree on how exactly to parse a query is not something we want), see https://www.oxeye.io/resources/golang-parameter-smuggling-attack for more details. For the *SubjectAccessReview endpoints of the kube-apiserver: * If rawSelector is empty and requirements are empty, the request is not limited. * If rawSelector is present and requirements are empty, the rawSelector will be parsed and limited if the parsing succeeds. * If rawSelector is empty and requirements are present, the requirements should be honored * If rawSelector is present and requirements are present, the request is invalid.

entries.title: LocalSubjectAccessReview
entries.summary: LocalSubjectAccessReview checks whether or not a user or group can perform an action in a given namespace. Having a namespace scoped resource makes it much easier to grant namespace scoped policy that includes permissions checking.

entries.title: NonResourceAttributes
entries.summary: NonResourceAttributes includes the authorization attributes available for non-resource requests to the Authorizer interface

entries.title: NonResourceRule
entries.summary: NonResourceRule holds information that describes a rule for the non-resource

entries.title: ResourceAttributes
entries.summary: ResourceAttributes includes the authorization attributes available for resource requests to the Authorizer interface

entries.title: ResourceRule
entries.summary: ResourceRule is the list of actions the subject is allowed to perform on resources. The list ordering isn't significant, may contain duplicates, and possibly be incomplete.

entries.title: SelfSubjectAccessReview
entries.summary: SelfSubjectAccessReview checks whether or the current user can perform an action.  Not filling in a spec.namespace means "in all namespaces".  Self is a special case, because users should always be able to check whether they can perform an action
